_notes: Windkey JSON plugin. Covers 43 of 52 parameters. Added: masterTuning (nibble SysEx), bcOnOff (CC11 inverted), scaleTuning C#-C (12-byte multi_sysex). Omitted: tvfVelocity (reserved/no MIDI), temperament (UI-only preset cascade). Protocol: Roland GS SysEx header F0 41 00 42 12 40; NRPN (CC99+CC98+CC6); CC; Program Change.
name: Windkey
author: Gianluca Barbaro

entries.text: The Tahorng Windkey is a USB MIDI wind controller. This section documents all parameters configurable from Hemiola’s Configuration Tool tab.
Changes are sent to the device in real time but are not saved to its internal memory. Use Presets to store and recall your settings.
Instrument
Select one of 10 available voices: Melodica, Accordion, Harmonica, Reed Organ, Synth Lead, Strings, Sax, Clarinet, Flute, Recorder.
Breath Controller
Enables or disables breath-sensor control of expression. When on, blowing harder increases volume and expressiveness; when off, volume is fixed.
Volume
Overall MIDI output level (0 – 10).
Transpose
Shifts all notes up or down in semitones (−24 to +24). 0 = no shift, positive = higher pitch, negative = lower pitch.
MIDI
Velocity Slope – maps playing force to note volume. Lower values give a softer, more even response; higher values make playing more dynamic.
Velocity Offset – shifts the baseline loudness. Raise this if notes feel too quiet even when playing hard.
PB Range – sets how far the pitch bend moves in semitones.
Vibrato
Rate – how fast the pitch oscillates.
Depth – how wide the pitch oscillation is. Keep this low for a subtle effect.
Delay – how long after playing a note before vibrato starts.
TVF (Time-Variant Filter)
A low-pass filter that shapes the brightness of the sound over time.
Cutoff – the frequency above which harmonics are rolled off. Lower = darker sound; higher = brighter sound.
Resonance – emphasises frequencies around the cutoff, adding a characteristic peak.
TVF Envelope
Controls how the filter opens and closes around each note.
Attack – how quickly filter brightness rises at the start of a note.
Decay – how quickly the brightness drops after the initial peak.
Release – how quickly the filter closes after releasing the note.
Centre value (64) is the neutral position for all Synth parameters.
EQ
EQ Enable – master on/off switch for the equaliser.
Brightness – overall tonal brightness (0–100). Works closely with the EQ parameters.
Low Gain – boosts or cuts bass frequencies (in dB).
Low Freq – the frequency around which the bass adjustment is applied (up to 800 Hz).
High Gain – boosts or cuts treble frequencies (in dB).
High Freq – the frequency around which the treble adjustment is applied (up to 5.8 kHz).
Reverb
Select a reverb type: Room 1/2/3, Hall 1/2, Plate, Delay, Pan Delay.
Level – how much reverb is added to the dry signal.
Character – tonal colour of the space (darker to brighter).
Pre-LPF – softens the reverb input; higher values give a warmer, less bright tail.
Master Level – overall output level of the reverb effect.
Time – how long the reverb tail lingers.
Pre-delay – a short pause before the first reflection, giving a sense of room size.
Feedback – tail density for reverb types; repeat count for delay types.
Chorus
Select a chorus type: Chorus 1–4, Feedback, Flanger, Short Delay, FB Delay.
Level – output level of the chorus effect.
Send – how much of the chorus signal is sent to the reverb unit (0–127).
Feedback – amount of processed signal fed back into the chorus; more = stronger, swirling effect.
Delay – base delay time of the chorus modulation; longer values give a wider, more spread-out sound.
Rate – how fast the modulation oscillates.
Depth – how wide the pitch/delay modulation swings.
Pre-LPF – low-pass filter before the chorus; higher values cut more high-frequency content entering the effect.
Master Tuning
Fine-tunes the entire instrument in cents (−100 to +100 ¢). 0 ¢ corresponds to the standard A4 = 440 Hz.
Temperament
Selects a historical or specialised tuning system. Choosing a temperament automatically calculates and sends all 12 per-note scale-tuning values.
Equal Tempered (standard) and several stretched variants.
Just Tempered (Schugk, Barbour) – naturally harmonious intervals.
Pythagorean and its many fifth-chain transpositions.
Meantone, Comma-based, and historical organ temperaments (Silbermann, Manderscheidt, etc.).
Per-note Scale Tuning
Each of the 12 chromatic notes (C through B) has its own knob showing the offset in cents (−63 to +63 ¢) relative to equal temperament.
Adjusting any knob overrides the selected temperament. Selecting a temperament replaces all 12 per-note values; further edits are applied on top.
